KPI, just to define that, is the minimum required number the rep needs to hit for you to be able to scale. So I've also seen that people tend to set their KPIs way too high. Like maybe they're closing 45%. They're like, well, my rep needs to close 40 or 45%. Or my rep's doing this full time. He needs to close more than me. He needs to close 55%. But look, if you're going to be able to hit, let's say, 6 to 8x ROAS with 25% closing ratio, then really, if that person's closing 25% and you're hitting 6 to 8x ROAS based on that person, then that's fine. Like that's all you need to be able to scale. So that's a performing asset for you. Now that doesn't mean that you don't want to coach them and try to get them better to hit 30, 40, and more. Just remember, KPI is just the minimum we need to get that person profitable within the returns that we want. So we don't want to be setting the KPIs way too high and firing people that otherwise would have been pretty good. Doesn't mean they have to stay at 25. KPI is just the minimum required. They need to be able to stay on the team, which is also the minimum they need to hit for you to be able to scale. So we're just aiming for good enough here. Now phase two is optimization and regulation. Two outcomes here is it taken from KPI. Then to maximizing their potential. So getting them from 25% to 40%, 50%, 60%, whatever.
